examples.title: Simple Input-Output Program
examples.description: A basic program demonstrating input, processing, and output in Python
examples.code:
# Simple Python Program - Greeting User
# This program takes user name and age, displays greeting

# Get user input
name = input("Enter your name: ")
age = input("Enter your age: ")

# Process data
age = int(age)  # Convert string to integer
birth_year = 2024 - age

# Output result
print("\n--- Welcome! ---")
print("Hello,", name)
print("You are", age, "years old")
print("You were born in:", birth_year)
print("-------------------")
examples.explanation: This program demonstrates: input() for reading user data, int() for type conversion, string concatenation in print(), and basic arithmetic operations.

examples.title: Arithmetic Operations Demo
examples.description: Demonstrates all arithmetic operators in Python
examples.code:
# Arithmetic Operators in Python

# Define two numbers
a = 15
b = 4

# Perform arithmetic operations
print("Given: a =", a, ", b =", b)
print("-" * 30)

print("a + b  =", a + b, "    # Addition")
print("a - b  =", a - b, "    # Subtraction")
print("a * b  =", a * b, "    # Multiplication")
print("a / b  =", a / b, "    # Division (float)")
print("a // b =", a // b, "   # Floor Division")
print("a % b  =", a % b, "    # Modulus (remainder)")
print("a ** b =", a ** b, "   # Exponent (power)")

# Demonstrate operator precedence
result = 2 + 3 * 4
print("\n2 + 3 * 4 =", result, "# Multiplication before Addition")
result = (2 + 3) * 4
print("(2 + 3) * 4 =", result, "# Parentheses override precedence")
examples.explanation: Shows all 7 arithmetic operators: +, -, *, /, //, %, **. Also demonstrates operator precedence using PEMDAS rule.

examples.title: Type Conversion Examples
examples.description: Program showing explicit type conversion between different data types
examples.code:
# Type Conversion in Python

# String to Integer/Float
num_str = "25"
num_int = int(num_str)       # "25" → 25
num_float = float(num_str)   # "25" → 25.0
print("String '25' → int:", num_int, "| type:", type(num_int).__name__)
print("String '25' → float:", num_float, "| type:", type(num_float).__name__)

# Integer/Float to String
price = 99.99
price_str = str(price)
print("\nFloat 99.99 → String:", price_str, "| type:", type(price_str).__name__)

# Integer to Float (automatic in operations)
x = 5
y = 2
result = x / y
print("\n5 / 2 =", result, "| type:", type(result).__name__)

# Using bool()
print("\nTruthy/Falsey values:")
print("bool(1):", bool(1), "| bool(0):", bool(0))
print("bool('Hi'):", bool('Hi'), "| bool(''):", bool(''))
print("bool([ ]):", bool([]), "| bool([1]):", bool([1]))

# User input with conversion
age = int(input("\nEnter your age: "))
print("Age squared:", age ** 2)
examples.explanation: Demonstrates type conversion functions: int(), float(), str(), bool(). Shows implicit vs explicit conversion and how different values evaluate to True/False in boolean context.

examples.title: String Formatting Program
examples.description: Demonstrates different ways to format output in Python
examples.code:
# String Formatting in Python

name = "Alice"
age = 21
marks = 85.567

# Method 1: Concatenation with str()
print("Method 1 - Concatenation:")
print("Name: " + name + ", Age: " + str(age))

# Method 2: % formatting (old style)
print("\nMethod 2 - % formatting:")
print("Name: %s, Age: %d, Marks: %.2f" % (name, age, marks))

# Method 3: format() method
print("\nMethod 3 - format() method:")
print("Name: {}, Age: {}, Marks: {:.2f}".format(name, age, marks))
print("Name: {0}, Age: {1}, Marks: {2:.2f}".format(name, age, marks))

# Method 4: f-strings (Python 3.6+)
print("\nMethod 4 - f-strings:")
print(f"Name: {name}, Age: {age}, Marks: {marks:.2f}")
print(f"Age after 10 years: {age + 10}")

# Escape sequences demo
print("\n--- Escape Sequences ---")
print("Newline\n here")
print("Tab\there")
print("Backslash: \\")
print('Quote: \'single\"')
examples.explanation: Shows 4 methods of string formatting with different syntax. Also demonstrates escape sequences for special character output. f-strings are most modern and recommended.
